1: Isaiah 40:1 opens the second major section of Isaiah (Isaiah 40-66) with the most famous comfort-oracle in the OT: 'Nachamu nachamu ammi yomar Eloheikhem, Comfort, comfort my people, says your God.' The double nachamu (comfort, comfort = the double imperative of urgency and intensity, matching the double 'chaos and void' of Genesis 1:2 that this section will reverse) is addressed to unspecified heralds ('nachamu' is masculine plural = comfort! [you all]). The theological pivot: Isaiah 39 ends with the Babylonian exile decreed; Isaiah 40 begins with comfort for the exiles. The gap between the decree (Isaiah 39:6-7) and the comfort (Isaiah 40:1) is the exile itself — the narrative leaps over the 115 years of Israel's history between 701 BCE and 586 BCE to address the community already in exile. 'My people' (ammi) = the covenant relationship still intact despite the exile; 'your God' (Eloheikhem) = the covenant formula maintained.
2: 'Speak tenderly to Jerusalem and declare to her that her time of service is ended, that her iniquity is pardoned, that she has received from the LORD's hand double for all her sins' — the three declarations of comfort: 'dabber al-lev Yerushalayim, speak to the heart of Jerusalem' (the intimate speech of a comforting parent or spouse — the same 'speaking to the heart' as Genesis 34:3 when Shechem spoke tenderly to Dinah; Hosea 2:14 when YHWH speaks tenderly to Israel in the wilderness). (1) 'ki maleah tseva'ah, for her hard service is completed' (tsava = military/forced service, the conscripted labor of the exile); (2) 'ki nirtsah avonah, for her iniquity is pardoned/accepted' (nirtsah from ratsah = to be pleased with, to accept — the exile has been the atonement for the sin, YHWH now 'accepting' it as complete); (3) 'ki laqechah miyyad YHWH kiflayim bechol-chataoteiyha, for she has received from YHWH's hand double for all her sins' — the kiflayim (double = a full, complete measure; the legal payment of double for theft, Exodus 22:7).
3: 'A voice cries in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the LORD; make straight in the desert a highway for our God' — the herald's command for the divine processional road: 'qol qore bamidbar, a voice crying in the wilderness.' The unidentified 'voice' (qol) calling in the wilderness. The command: 'panu derekh YHWH, yasshiru ba'aravah mesillah le'Elohenu, prepare the way of YHWH; make level in the desert a highway for our God.' The ANE royal road-preparation: before a king traveled, his advance team would level the road, fill potholes, smooth rough sections. The wilderness/desert as the terrain: the return from Babylon would traverse the Syrian desert. The theological dimension: YHWH himself is coming to lead his people home, and the road is prepared for the divine warrior-shepherd. All four Gospels apply Isaiah 40:3 to John the Baptist (Matthew 3:3; Mark 1:3; Luke 3:4; John 1:23): he is the 'voice crying in the wilderness' preparing the way for Jesus.
4: 'Every valley will be raised up and every mountain and hill leveled; the crooked will be made straight and the rough ground a plain' — the cosmic road-preparation: 'kol-gey yinnase vekhol-har vegiv'ah yishpalu, every valley will be lifted up and every mountain and hill will be leveled.' The radical terraforming: valleys raised (the low places of depression elevated), mountains leveled (the high barriers removed), crooked made straight (the devious paths straightened), rough ground smoothed. This is not merely topographical but theological: the obstacles between the exiles and their God are removed. Luke 3:5-6 quotes the full version, extending to 'all flesh will see God's salvation' (Isaiah 40:5). The theology of the prepared road: YHWH does not require his people to navigate the obstacles; he clears them. Grace precedes the journey.
5: 'The glory of the LORD will be revealed, and all flesh will see it together; for the mouth of the LORD has spoken' — the purpose and guarantee of the preparation: 'venighah kevod YHWH vera'u khol-basar yachdav, and the glory of YHWH will be revealed and all flesh will see it together.' The kavod (glory = the radiant presence-manifestation of YHWH, the shekinah-glory of Exodus 16:10; Isaiah 24:16-17; Isaiah 40:34-35 when YHWH's cloud-glory filled the tabernacle). 'All flesh together' (kol-basar yachdav = every human being simultaneously) = the universal, simultaneous revelation of YHWH's glory in the new Exodus event. The guarantee: 'ki pi YHWH dibber, for the mouth of YHWH has spoken' — the prophetic authentication formula. YHWH's speech is the guarantee of the event: his word creates what it declares (Gen 1: 'God said... and there was').
6: 'A voice says, Cry! And someone asks, What shall I cry? All flesh is grass, and all its beauty is like the flower of the field' — the herald's commission and the human-transience message: 'qol omer qera, a voice says: cry out.' A different 'voice' from v3 now gives the second command. 'And he says, What shall I cry?' (veyomar mah aqra, and the one commanded says: what shall I cry?) — the dialogic form: the commissioned herald asks for content. The content: 'kol-habasar chatsir vekol-chasdoh ke'tsits hassadeh, all flesh is grass and all its faithfulness/beauty is like the flower of the field.' The chatsir (grass/hay) and tsits hassadeh (flower of the field) = the vegetation that is beautiful in its brief flowering but rapidly fades. 1 Peter 1:24-25 quotes Isaiah 40:6-8 directly: 'All flesh is like grass and all human glory is like the flower of the field; the grass withers and the flower falls, but the word of the Lord endures forever.'
7: 'The grass withers and the flower fades when the breath of the LORD blows on them; the people are surely grass' — the mechanism of human transience: 'yavesh chatsir naval tsits ki ruach YHWH nashevah bo, the grass withers, the flower fades, for the breath of YHWH blows on them.' The ruach YHWH (the breath/spirit/wind of YHWH) is the divine wind that in Genesis 1:2 'hovered over the waters' and in Genesis 2:7 'breathed into man's nostrils the breath of life.' The same divine breath that creates life also, when blown, withers the grass of human pretension. 'The people are surely grass' (aken chatsir ha'am, certainly the people are grass) — the emphatic aken (surely/certainly) applies the botanical metaphor directly to human beings. All the royal pride, military power, and accumulated wealth of the nations that oppose YHWH are as durable as summer grass under a scorching wind.
8: 'The grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our God stands forever' — the central contrast of the herald's message: 'yavesh chatsir naval tsits, udvar Elohenu yaqum le'olam, the grass withers, the flower fades, but the word of our God stands forever.' The contrast could not be sharper: the transient (grass, flower) vs. the permanent (YHWH's word). The 'word of our God' (dvar Elohenu = the prophetic-covenantal word, the creative word of Gen 1, the Torah, and the specific promises of Isaiah 40:1-5) 'stands forever' (yaqum le'olam = rises/stands to the age = is permanently established). The comfort for the exiles who have watched everything human collapse (Jerusalem, the temple, the Davidic monarchy, the national identity) is that the divine word remains. What was promised remains promised; what was guaranteed remains guaranteed despite the collapse of every human institution.
9: 'Get up onto a high mountain, O Zion, messenger of good news! Lift your voice with strength, O Jerusalem, herald of good news! Lift it up; do not fear! Say to the cities of Judah: Look — your God!' — the herald's proclamation to Zion: 'al-har-gavoah ali-lakh mevasseret Tsiyon, onto a high mountain go up for yourself, herald of Zion.' The mevasseret (herald of good news = from bissar = to bring good news, the root of the NT euangelion/gospel). Zion is the herald who proclaims the good news to the cities of Judah. 'Lift your voice with strength' (harimi vekoach qolekh mevasseret Yerushalayim, lift up your voice with strength, herald of Jerusalem). 'Do not fear' (al-tira'i) = the standard comfort formula now applied to the community-herald. The content of the proclamation: 'hineh Eloheikhem, behold your God!' — the recognition formula: the God who has returned is the covenant God ('your God'), not an abstract divine principle. The heralding of YHWH's return is the first gospel.
10: 'See — the Lord GOD comes with strength, and his arm rules on his behalf; see — his reward is with him, and his recompense before him' — the divine warrior's arrival: 'hineh Adonai YHWH bechazaq yavo uzerou moshelah lo, behold the Lord YHWH comes with strength, and his arm rules for him.' The divine warrior's arm (zero'ah = the arm of strength, the instrument of the Exodus: Exodus 6:6; Deuteronomy 4:34; Isaiah 5:15; Isaiah 26:8) rules 'for him' (lo = on his behalf). 'His reward is with him' (hineh sekharo itto, behold his wage/reward is with him) = the wages YHWH pays to his redeemed: the return from exile, the restoration of covenant fellowship, the multiplication of blessing. 'And his recompense before him' (upe'ulato lefanaiv, and his work/recompense before him) — the divine warrior both brings the reward to his people and carries the recompense for the enemies before him as he advances.
11: 'He tends his flock like a shepherd; he gathers the lambs in his arm and carries them in his bosom; he gently leads the nursing ewes' — the divine shepherd: 'kero'eh edro yir'eh, like a shepherd he tends his flock.' The warrior of v10 is immediately revealed as the shepherd of v11 — the two images held together describe the one God who fights for his people and tenderly cares for the weak. 'He gathers the lambs in his arm' (bizeroo yiqabbets telaim, in his arm he gathers the lambs) — the arm (zero'ah) that is the instrument of the warrior (v10) is the same arm that gathers the vulnerable lambs. 'Carries them in his bosom' (uvecheiqo yissa, and carries them in his bosom/breast) — the intimate carrying of an infant. 'Gently leads the nursing ewes' (allot yenachel = the nursing ewes, the mothers with young, he will gently lead) — the most vulnerable part of the flock (nursing mothers with lambs) receive the most careful guidance. John 10:11-14 makes the 'Good Shepherd' imagery explicit in Jesus; John 10:27-29 has Jesus carrying his sheep securely.
12: 'Who has measured the waters in the hollow of his hand, marked off the heavens with a span, gathered up the dust of the earth in a measure, weighed the mountains in scales and the hills in a balance?' — the incomparability hymn's five creation-mastery questions: (1) 'mi-madad veshipchulelo mayim, who has measured the waters in the hollow of his hand?' — the world's oceans held in a cupped palm; (2) 'vesamayim bazzeret tikken, and the heavens he measured with a span?' — the span (zereth = the hand-width from thumb to little finger) measuring the entire sky; (3) 'vekhal beshalis afer ha'arets, and the dust of the earth in a third-measure?' — the world's soil measured in a one-third unit; (4) 'veshaqal behapeles harim, and weighed the mountains in scales?' — the Himalayan and Alpine ranges in a weighing balance; (5) 'ugevaot bemozenayim, and the hills in a balance?' — the hills in a merchant's scale. All five questions expect the same answer: no one but YHWH. The rhetorical questions establish the category difference between YHWH and all created powers.
13: 'Who has guided the Spirit of the LORD, or who has been his counselor to instruct him?' — the divine self-sufficiency in wisdom: 'mi-tikken et-ruach YHWH, who has directed/measured the Spirit of YHWH?' The tikken (measured/directed = from tikken = to establish/measure, the same root as the measuring of v12) applied to YHWH's ruach = no one can direct or measure the divine Spirit. The parallel: 'veish atsato yodi'enu, and a man of his counsel to instruct him?' — no human advisor is in YHWH's council to instruct him. Paul quotes v13 in Romans 11:34: 'Who has known the mind of the Lord? Or who has been his counselor?' — and in 1 Corinthians 2:16: 'Who has known the mind of the Lord so as to instruct him? But we have the mind of Christ.' The Johannine Logos-theology and Pauline Christology both draw on this verse to explain how the pre-existent wisdom/word of YHWH could be incarnate in Jesus.
14: 'With whom did he consult? Who instructed him and taught him the path of justice, who taught him knowledge and showed him the way of understanding?' — the four wisdom-attributes YHWH possesses independently: (1) mishpat (the path of justice), (2) da'at (knowledge), (3) tevunah (the way of understanding/discernment). No one taught YHWH any of these — he possesses them as intrinsic attributes of his nature, not as acquired learning. The theological implication for the exiles: the God who brings them comfort is not learning or improvising as he goes. His rescue plan is not reactive but based on complete wisdom ('plans formed long ago,' Isaiah 25:1). The incomparability of YHWH's wisdom contrasts with the 'wisdom of the wise' that will perish (Isaiah 29:14) and the 'wisdom of Egypt' that is worthless (Isaiah 31:2).
15: 'See — the nations are like a drop in a bucket and are counted as dust on the scales; see — he lifts up the coastlands like a grain of fine dust' — the nations' insignificance before the creator: 'hen goyim kemar meddeli, behold the nations are like a drop from a bucket.' The mar meddeli (a drop from a bucket = the water droplet on the outside of a bucket after it has been dipped = the most insignificant possible residue). 'And counted as dust on the scales' (ucheshab avaq moznayim, and accounted as fine dust of the scales = the weightless fine powder that settles on the balance-pans between weighings). 'He lifts up the coastlands like a grain of fine dust' (hen iyyim kaddiq yittol, the coastlands he lifts up like a very fine thing) — the Mediterranean coastlands (the entire western horizon of the ANE political world) = a grain of fine dust lifted by YHWH. The nations that Sennacherib boasted of conquering are weightless specks before their creator.
16: 'Even Lebanon is not sufficient for firewood, nor are its animals enough for a burnt offering' — the transcendence of YHWH beyond adequate worship: 'uLevanon ein dai ber, and Lebanon is not sufficient for fuel.' Lebanon (the great cedar-forest whose timber was the most prized in the ANE — used for Solomon's temple, 1 Kgs 5; Sennacherib's boast in 37:24 about cutting its cedars) would not provide enough wood to burn an adequate offering to YHWH. 'Nor are its animals enough for a burnt offering' (vechayato ein dei olah, and its animals are not sufficient for a burnt offering). The entire forested mountain and its abundant wildlife cannot provide a sacrifice worthy of YHWH. The logic: if all of Lebanon's timber and animals cannot adequately honor YHWH, then no physical sacrifice — however grand — can encompass his worth. This is not an attack on sacrifice but an affirmation of YHWH's incomparable transcendence.
17: 'All the nations are as nothing before him; they are counted by him as less than nothing and futility' — the superlative of insignificance: 'kol-hagoyim ke'ayin negdo, all the nations are as nothing before him.' The ke'ayin (as nothing = like non-being, like the ayin that is the absence of existence) — the most extreme possible statement of insignificance. 'They are counted by him as less than nothing and futility' (me'efes uvohu nechshevoo lo, from nothing and chaos they are reckoned to him). The me'efes (from nothing = less than zero) and tohu (chaos/void = the tohu of Genesis 1:2) — the nations in YHWH's accounting are below zero, less than the pre-creation chaos. This is not contempt for human beings but a systematic demonstration of the infinite category-difference between the creator and any collection of creatures. Against the Babylonian imperial theology that treated nations as components of a divine order, YHWH is categorically above and beyond the entire national system.
18: 'To whom will you compare God? What likeness will you set beside him?' — the incomparability question as the threshold of the idol polemic: 've'el-mi tedamyun El umah-demut ta'arkhu lo, and to whom will you liken God, and what comparison will you set beside him?' The central question of the entire incomparability section (Isaiah 40:12-26). The 'likeness' (demuth = the image/resemblance, the same word as Genesis 1:26: 'let us make humanity in our image and likeness') = what comparison class does YHWH belong to? The answer: none. He is sui generis — without comparison. The rhetorical question opens into the idol polemic (Isaiah 40:19-20) which provides the false answer (an idol) and then exposes its absurdity. The same incomparability question returns in Isaiah 40:25 ('To whom will you compare me? Who is my equal? says the Holy One').
19: 'A craftsman casts an idol, and the goldsmith overlays it with gold and fashions silver chains for it' — the idol-making process analyzed: 'happesel nassakh charash, the craftsman has poured out/cast an idol.' The pesel (the idol, from pasal = to hew/carve) made by the charash (the craftsman/smith = the skilled metalworker). The goldsmith (tsaref) overlays it with gold (yetsappenhu zahav, he covers it with gold) and fashions chains of silver (uretuqot keseph yetsarref, silver chains he works). The contrast: YHWH 'created the heavens and the earth' (v12-17's implied creator of all creation) vs. the craftsman who takes created materials (gold, silver) and manufactures a decorated object. The creator of gold vs. an object covered with gold = the absurdity of confusing the creator with a precious-metal-covered wooden/stone form. Isaiah 44:9-20 develops the idol-polemic to its most extended satirical form.
20: 'He who is too poor to bring a proper offering selects wood that will not rot; he looks for a skilled craftsman to set up an idol that will not topple' — the poor man's idol: 'hamseukan terumah ets lo-yirqav yivchar, the one who is too poor for an offering — wood that will not rot he chooses.' The 'poor man's idol': even the person who cannot afford gold or silver wants an idol, so he selects good-quality wood (that will resist decay) rather than precious metals. 'He seeks a skilled craftsman' (charash chakham yevaqesh-lo, he seeks for himself a skilled craftsman) 'to set up an idol that will not topple' (hakhim pesel bilthi yimot, to set up a carved image so it will not topple). The irony: the craftsman's primary technical challenge is making the idol stand up without falling — a deity whose main quality is structural stability. The 'idol that won't topple' becomes a marker of divine weakness: YHWH holds the heavens in place; this deity needs a craftsman to ensure it doesn't fall over.
21: 'Do you not know? Have you not heard? Has it not been declared to you from the beginning? Have you not understood from the foundations of the earth?' — the four rhetorical questions of prophetic pedagogy: (1) 'halo tede'u, do you not know?' (2) 'halo tishme'u, do you not hear?' (3) 'halo huggad mero'sh lakhem, has it not been declared to you from the beginning?' (4) 'halo havvinu mosdot ha'arets, have you not understood the foundations of the earth?' The four questions bracket human knowing: active knowledge (know), receptive knowledge (hear), revealed knowledge (declared), foundational knowledge (from the earth's foundations). The implied answer to all four: yes, it has been known, heard, declared, and foundationally available — the exiles have access to this knowledge, they have simply failed to apply it. The teaching that follows (Isaiah 40:22-26) supplies the content they should already know.
22: 'It is he who sits enthroned above the circle of the earth, whose inhabitants are like grasshoppers; who stretches out the heavens like a curtain and spreads them like a tent to live in' — YHWH's cosmic governance: 'haggoisher al-chug ha'arets veyyosheveyha kechaggavim, he who sits above the circle/disk of the earth, and its inhabitants are like grasshoppers.' The chug (circle = the disk or vault of the sky or earth, from chug = to draw a circle; also Job 22:14: 'he walks in the vault of heaven'; Proverbs 8:27: 'when he inscribed a circle on the face of the deep'). The inhabitants as grasshoppers (chaggavim = locusts/grasshoppers) = the perspective from YHWH's cosmic height makes even the massed nations look like insects. 'He stretches out the heavens like a curtain' (hannotteh kashaqqim shamayim, the one who spreads out the heavens like a thin curtain/veil) and 'spreads them like a tent' (vayimtahem kaohel lasheveth, and stretches them out as a tent for dwelling) = the creation-imagery of the sky as YHWH's dwelling-tent (Psalms 104:2-3).
23: 'He brings princes to nothing and reduces the rulers of the earth to emptiness' — the divine reversal of political power: 'hannoten rozenim la'ayin, he gives princes to nothingness.' The rozenim (the rulers/princes = those who deliberate in council, the senior governmental figures) are given 'to nothingness' (la'ayin = to the non-being of v17). The parallel: 'shofetei erets kehahu asah, judges/rulers of the earth like a chaos/void he makes them.' The tohu (chaos/void = the Genesis 1:2 pre-creation state) applied to earthly rulers — YHWH reduces the great to the condition of pre-creation. This is the Day-of-the-LORD theology (Isaiah 2:11-17: 'the loftiness of man will be bowed down, and the haughtiness of men will be humbled') applied to the Babylonian empire that held Israel in exile.
24: 'Scarcely have they been planted, scarcely sown, their root scarcely taken in the earth, when he blows on them and they wither, and the whirlwind sweeps them away like chaff' — the rapid dissolution of earthly power: 'af bilthi-nittanu af bilthi-zurou af bilthi-sherash ba'aretz giz'am, scarcely planted, scarcely sown, scarcely their stem rooted in the earth.' The three 'scarcely' (af bilthi, even/also not yet) = the three stages of plant growth (planted, sown from seed, root-established) all barely reached before the ending comes. 'And he blows on them and they wither' (veyiggash bahem vayyiveshu, and he blows on them and they wither) — the divine breath (neshimah/ruach = YHWH's blowing) as the withering agent (v7: 'the breath of YHWH blows on them; the people are surely grass'). 'The whirlwind sweeps them away like chaff' (useara kaqash yissaem, like chaff a whirlwind takes them) — the final dissolution: not gradually decaying but suddenly swept away.
25: 'To whom will you compare me? Who is my equal? says the Holy One' — the incomparability question repeated with self-affirmation: 'veal-mi tedamyeuni ve'eshveh yomar qadosh, to whom will you liken me, and I am equal? says the Holy One.' The first incomparability question (v18) asked from outside; now the Holy One (qadosh = YHWH in his distinct, set-apart uniqueness, Isaiah's distinctive title) asks it himself from within — the divine self-assertion. The 'Holy One' without the usual 'of Israel' = the absolute holiness of YHWH in his own being, not merely as Israel's covenant God. The question 've'eshveh, and I am equal to?' expects the same answer as all the preceding rhetorical questions: nothing and no one.
26: 'Lift up your eyes on high and see: who created all these? He leads out their army by number, calling each one by name. Because of his great power and mighty strength, not one of them is missing' — the night-sky as proof of YHWH's incomparability: 'se'u marom eineikhem ur'u mi-vara elleh, lift up your eyes on high and see: who created these?' The starry night sky as the answer to the incomparability question. The stars as YHWH's army: 'hamotsi bemissphar tseva'am lekhullam beshem yiqra, who brings out by number their host, calls them all by name.' The Babylonian astrology assigned divine powers and names to stars — the zodiac as a divine system governing human fate. Isaiah's counter-theology: YHWH is not a star; he calls the stars by name as a military commander calls his troops. 'Not one of them is missing' (ish lo ne'dar, not one is absent) = YHWH's control over the stars is total — perfect military discipline. The exiles in Babylon, surrounded by star-worship, are shown that the stars they see are YHWH's army, not their masters.
27: 'Why do you say, O Jacob, and why do you speak, O Israel: My way is hidden from the LORD, and my cause is overlooked by my God?' — the complaint of divine hiddenness addressed: 'lamah tomar Ya'aqov utedabber Yisrael, why do you say, Jacob, and speak, Israel?' The double name (Jacob/Israel = the covenantal and national identity simultaneously) addressed in the complaint: 'nistarah darkhi meYHWH, my way is hidden from YHWH.' The hidden way (derekh nistar = the path that YHWH cannot see) = the exilic complaint that YHWH has lost track of them in Babylon. 'And my justice/cause is overlooked by my God' (umishpati me'Elohay ya'avor, and my legal cause passes by my God unnoticed). The legal complaint: mishpat (the legal case/right) that YHWH has missed. The exiles feel invisible — their suffering unobserved, their legal claim forgotten. The rhetorical question ('why do you say?') implies the complaint is incorrect, and Isaiah 40:28-31 provide the correction.
28: 'Do you not know? Have you not heard? The LORD is the everlasting God, the Creator of the ends of the earth. He does not grow faint or weary; his understanding cannot be searched out' — the correction of the hiddenness-complaint with the doctrine of divine eternity and inexhaustibility: 'halo yadatah im-lo shamata, do you not know, or have you not heard?' The same two rhetorical questions as v21 — the exiles are being reminded of what they should already know. 'YHWH Elohei olam bore qetsot ha'arets, YHWH is the God of eternity, the creator of the ends of the earth.' The olam (eternity/everlasting) applied to YHWH = he is not subject to the time-limitations that cause human beings to forget or become exhausted. 'He does not grow faint or weary' (lo yiy'af velo yiga, he does not faint and does not grow weary) — unlike the human beings who grow faint (v29-30), YHWH is exempt from fatigue. 'His understanding cannot be searched out' (ve'en cheqer livunato, and there is no searching out of his understanding) = the divine wisdom is inexhaustible.
29: 'He gives strength to the weary and increases the power of those who have no might' — the transfer of divine strength to the exhausted: 'noten laye'ef koach, he gives to the weary strength.' The noten (he gives = continuous present action, not a past event) characterizes YHWH's ongoing sustaining of the weak. The ye'ef (weary/faint = those exhausted by the demands of the exile, the sustained suffering of displacement) receive koach (strength = the capacity for effective action). 'And increases the power of those who have no might' (ulein onim atsumah yarbeh, and to those without strength he multiplies power). The 'no might' (ein onim = literally 'there is no strength') = those at absolute zero of energy, the completely depleted. YHWH multiplies (yarbeh = increases, makes many) their power. The theological mechanism: the divine inexhaustibility (v28: 'he does not grow faint or weary') transfers to the human community. What YHWH has without limit, he gives to those who have run out.
30: 'Even youths grow faint and weary, and young men stumble and fall' — the contrast with human energy at its peak: 'veyif'u ne'arim veyig'au vebahurim kashol yikashelu, even the young men faint and grow weary, and the young warriors stumble and fall.' The ne'arim (youths = the young men at peak physical condition) and bahurim (the choice young men = the military-age male at peak fitness) both faint and stumble. The ba-hurim kashol yikashelu (the choice young men will stumble, stumbling) = the absolute infinitive creating emphatic certainty. Human energy is finite — even the strongest, fittest, most vital humans eventually run out. This is not pessimism but the realistic diagnosis that enables the promise of v31: the only alternative to eventual exhaustion is receiving strength from the inexhaustible God.
31: 'But those who wait for the LORD will renew their strength; they will soar on wings like eagles; they will run and not grow weary; they will walk and not faint' — the climax of the Comfort Oracle: 'vequvei YHWH yachaliphu koach, but those who wait/hope in YHWH will renew their strength.' The qavah (to wait/hope = from the root meaning 'cord/thread' — those who are tethered to YHWH by the cord of hope and patient expectation). 'Will renew their strength' (yachaliphu koach = will exchange/renew strength) — the chalaf (to exchange/replace/renew = to exchange depleted strength for fresh strength from YHWH). Three levels of movement: 'they will soar on wings like eagles' (ya'alu ever kaneshrim, they will mount up with wings like eagles = the highest, most effortless movement); 'they will run and not grow weary' (yarutsu velo yig'au, they will run and not be weary = sustained effort without fatigue); 'they will walk and not faint' (yelekhu velo yiy'afu, they will walk and not faint = the ordinary, plodding, day-to-day perseverance without exhaustion). The descending order (soar → run → walk) may be intentional: the most dramatic manifestation is easiest; the ordinary walk without fainting is the deepest spiritual achievement.
